text:
For decades, communications in Eastern Europe were understood through the paradigm of the propaganda state. Peter Kenez famously argued in 1985 that the Bolsheviks were as much innovators in their propaganda messages as they were in their aim to reshape human beings through political socialization. Propaganda was no marginal aspect of Soviet life. Rather than being seen as an antithesis to the newly established societal and economic structures, propaganda was celebrated by Eastern European socialist regimes as a modernizing tool instrumental to these changes. The focus on the propaganda state has tended to reduce complex developments in Soviet communication to a bipolar narrative of either forced conformity or complete alienation, with citizens seemingly reduced to automatons without interpersonal ties in civil society.

departing from conventional totalitarian characterisations of the Soviet and Eastern European systems, this special issue, based on research in formerly closed archives and employing recent methodological approaches, draws on a range of evidence, from films and political announcements through discussions of revolutionary change in schools and folk art, to mass television under Brezhnev and analyses of viewer responses and other forms of participatory cultural practice. The state owned the means of communication, but its audiences appeared to change over time; from the early days of the revolution in the much-observed agit-trains to mass television in the Brezhnev years, media technologies fashioned particular forms of public-ness and particular social relationships and networks of interpersonal communication. In particular, in the attempt to get close to the experience of everyday people in a system in which autonomy over private forms of activity, such as reading and listening to music and the media, was not in practice restricted as long as those preferences matched certain formal parameters, people dealt with common places – media sites of unchanging popularity from decade to decade, favourite performers and stories that resonated in the private spaces of the family, or routines of observation and emotional participation, also centred on family, such as viewing television, which marked particular locations, within the home and outside, as belonging to the world of intimate relations whilst also integrating that world to the official sphere of power and influence.

Case studies from stenographic politics and wartime Jewish family correspondence to subversive animation in late Soviet times and the “democratization” of the telephone explore how technology and media shaped life in socialist societies. Examining the interplay between technology and social practice, the contributions in this issue illuminate a complex history of Soviet and socialist communications that involved more than just the propaganda efforts of the state and the quiescence of its citizens. Rather they show a society in motion where technology and media were contested terrain where citizens argued over their role in relation to the state and to each other.
